coredata.dc:title: Facile strategy for synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of acetamiprid
coredata.dc:creator: Dan, Xu
Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
Haiyan, Zhu
Zhiguo, Gu
Guangli, Wang
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical properties of metal-graphene hybrid depend on size and dispersion of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets. The study reports a facile synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion. Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) are firmly bound on graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) and then combined with Ag+ to form stable Ag/His-GQD/GO complex. Followed by heating in N2 to produce Ag/His-GQD/G. The resulting hybrid offers well-defined three-dimensional structure, in which all Ag nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the surface of graphene sheets. Interestingly, the size and density of Ag nanoparticles can be effectively adjusted by varying mass ratio of His-GQD/GO or Ag+/GO. To construct on the aptasensor, aptamer of acetamiprid was connected to Ag nanoparticles via Ag-S bond in the hybrid immobilized on surface of glass carbon electrode. The architecture creates an excellent catalytic activity. The aptasensor based on Ag/His-GQD/G exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, specificity and stability for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid. A linear relationship between the peak current of differential pulse voltammetry and logarithm value of acetamiprid concentration was obtained in the range from 1.0 × 10−16 to 5.0 × 10−12 M with a detection limit of 4.0 × 10−17 M. The method has been successfully applied in detection of acetamiprid in tea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene-silver composite
Synthesis
Functional graphene quantum dots
Biosensor
Acetamiprid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), TEM image (B) (inset is HRTEM image) and elemental mappings of Ag and N elements (C), FTIR spectrum (D) and XRD pattern (E) of Ag/His-GQD/G.
SEM images of Ag/His-GQD/G products prepared by using the Ag+/GO mass ratio of 0.07 and the His-GQD/GO mass ratio of 0.2 (A), 0.4 (B), 0.8 (C) or 1.25 (D).
Relationship curve of the mass ratios of His-GQD/GO with particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles in Ag/His-GQD/G.
SEM images of Ag/His-GQD/G products prepared by using the His-GQD/GO mass ratio of 1.25 and the Ag+/GO mass ratio of 0.007 (A), 0.021 (B), 0.058 (C), 0.09 (D), 0.014 (E) or 0.22 (F).
Relationship curve of the mass ratios of Ag+/GO with particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles in Ag/His-GQD/G.

DPV curves of electrode modified with Ag/His-GQD/G prepared by using the His-GQD/GO mass ratio of 1.25 and the Ag+/GO mass ratio of 0.07, 0.058, 0.14, 0.021, 0.007 and 0.22 (from top to bottom) in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3.
The protocol to construct on electrochemical sensing platform for detection of acetamiprid.

CV curves (A) and EIS curves (B) of the bare GCE, Ag/His-GQD/G/GCE, aptamer/Ag/His-GQD/G/GCE and MCH/aptamer/Ag/His-GQD/G/GCE in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. Inset: Equivalent circuit from the EIS measurements.
CV curves (A) and DPV curves (B) of aptasensor before and after incubated in 1.0 pM of acetamiprid for 50 min in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3.
Relationship curves of aptamer concentration (A) and incubation time with the ΔIp caused by 1.0 pM acetamiprid in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3.

A: DPV curves of aptasensor incubated in 1.0 × 10−16, 5.0 × 10−16, 1.0 × 10−15, 3.0 × 10−15, 1.0 × 10−14, 2.5 × 10−14, 6.0 × 10−14, 1.0 × 10−13, 2.5 × 10−13, 5.0 × 10−13, 1.0 × 10−12, 2.5 × 10−12 or 5.0 × 10−12 M of acetamiprid in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3 (from top to bottom). B: The relationship curve between DPV peak current and logarithm of acetamiprid concentration.
ΔIp value caused by incubation in 5.0 × 10−14 M of ACE, PAR, IMI, CAR or PEN.
Sensitivity comparison of different analytical techniques for acetamiprid detection.
The results of determination of acetamiprid in tea samples (N = 5).

head.ce:title: Facile strategy for synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of acetamiprid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical properties of metal-graphene hybrid depend on size and dispersion of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets. The study reports a facile synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion. Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) are firmly bound on graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) and then combined with Ag+ to form stable Ag/His-GQD/GO complex. Followed by heating in N2 to produce Ag/His-GQD/G. The resulting hybrid offers well-defined three-dimensional structure, in which all Ag nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the surface of graphene sheets. Interestingly, the size and density of Ag nanoparticles can be effectively adjusted by varying mass ratio of His-GQD/GO or Ag+/GO. To construct on the aptasensor, aptamer of acetamiprid was connected to Ag nanoparticles via Ag-S bond in the hybrid immobilized on surface of glass carbon electrode. The architecture creates an excellent catalytic activity. The aptasensor based on Ag/His-GQD/G exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, specificity and stability for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid. A linear relationship between the peak current of differential pulse voltammetry and logarithm value of acetamiprid concentration was obtained in the range from 1.0 × 10−16 to 5.0 × 10−12 M with a detection limit of 4.0 × 10−17 M. The method has been successfully applied in detection of acetamiprid in tea.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has been widely applied in many fields such as sensor, energy storage, catalysis and solar cell [1–3], because of its large surface area, high electrical conductivity and good chemical stability [4–6]. Recently, the applications of graphene as electrochemical sensing materials received more attention [7–10]. These methods always show high sensitivity. Nevertheless, two-dimensional graphene sheets are easy to occur a serious agglomeration via π-π stacking. This will lead to the loss of surface area [11]. To solve the problem, great effort has devoted to synthesis of three-dimensional graphene materials such as aerogel, sponge and foam [12–14]. Three-dimensional graphene offers the rich of channels for electron/ion transfer, large surface area and good mechanical performance [15]. These advantages make three-dimensional graphene more widely applied in construction of electrochemical sensors. However, graphene itself cannot bring the special catalysis. This greatly limits many applications of graphene.

Noble metal nanomaterial is mostly used sensing material to construct on electrochemical sensors because of its large surface area, special catalytic ability and high electrical conductivity [16,17] such as Au [18], Pt [19], Pd [20] and Ag [21]. Noble metal-graphene hybrids not only give an increased surface area, excellent binding capacity to analytes, but also exhibit synergistic catalytic effect of graphene and noble metal nanomaterial. The hybrid often produces a much better electrochemical performances compared with single graphene and single noble metal nanomaterial [22–24]. However, quite a few strategies were developed for synthesis of noble metal-graphene hybrids. Initially, hybrid of noble metal-graphene is achieved by mixing noble metal nanoparticles with graphene [25]. The strategy not only leads to the loss of noble metal nanoparticles during the washing process, but also causes a serious agglomeration of noble metal nanoparticles and graphene sheets due to weak binding force between graphene sheet and noble metal nanoparticle. To improve the mixing, many investigations adopt replacement of graphene by graphene oxide (GO) for synthesis of noble metal-graphene hybrid. For example, Ag-graphene is obtained by mixing AgNO3 with GO and subsequent reduction with NaBH4 [26]. The method greatly improves the binding strength between graphene and Ag nanoparticles, but the size of nanoparticles is difficult to be effectively controlled [27]. Our group reported a graphene aerogel-AuPd bimetallic nanoparticle composite [28]. In the synthesis, AuPd nanoparticles were made and then dispersed in graphene aerogel. The bimetallic nanoparticles show much higher catalytic activity than single Au or single Pd nanomaterial. In addition, our group also reported several hydrothermal and electrochemical strategies for synthesis of noble metal-graphene hybrids [29,30]. These investigations have demonstrated that the electrochemical properties of metal-graphene hybrid seriously depend on the size and dispersion of metal nanoparticles on the surface of graphene sheets [31]. However, present techniques are difficult to achieve controllable size and excellent dispersion of noble metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets.

In the study, we reported one strategy for synthesis of silver nanoparticles/histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots/graphene hybrid, which was termed as Ag/His-GQD/G. The as-synthesized hybrid shows a well-defined three-dimensional structure. All Ag nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the graphene sheets. The synthesis can achieve to regulate the size and density of Ag nanoparticles by varying the mass radio of His-GQD/GO or Ag+/GO. The aptasensor based on Ag/His-GQD/G exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, specificity and stability for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid. It has been successfully used for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid in tea samples.

The DNA probe (aptamer) (base sequence: 5′-(SH)-(CH2)6-TGT AAT TTG TCT GCA GCG GTT CTT GAT CGC TGA CAC CAT ATT ATG AAG A-3′) was purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Limited Company. Acetamiprid, methyl parathion, imidacloprid, carbaryl, sodium pentachlorophenol, 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), chitosan (CS), silver nitrate (AgNO3), histidine and citric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4) was prepared in the laboratory. The probe stock solution was prepared in the Tris/Mg/K buffer containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM KCI at pH 7.4, and stored at −20 °C. Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from natural graphite by the Hummers’ method [32]. The His-GQD was prepared by using a reported method in literature [33]. All aqueous solutions were prepared with distilled water, which was obtained from Milli-Q water purifying system.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were conducted on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectrums were measured by Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan).

GO (0.3 g) was dispersed in doubly distilled water (100 mL) to form uniform dispersion. Then, slowly added the His-GQD aqueous solution (0.375 g in 10 mL of doubly distilled water) into the above dispersion with stirring to form His-GQD/GO dispersion. After the acidity of dispersion was adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding 1 M NaOH aqueous solution, dropped 0.2 M AgNO3 (1.0 mL) under well stirring to produce Ag/His-GQD/GO complex. Followed by centrifugation at 5000 r/min and washing with doubly-distilled water for several times. The collected solid sample was dried in vacuum at 60 °C and then heated at 300 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h to obtain Ag/His-GQD/G. The above procedure was also used for the preparation of silver-graphene hybrid (Ag/G) only without His-GQD.

Glass carbon electrode (GCE, 2.0 mm in diameter) was polished with α-alumina powder of 0.05 mm on cloth, rinsed with doubly-distilled water in sequence and then sonically treated in doubly-distilled water and ethanol (1:1, V/V) for 10 min. Then, the GCE was subjected to the cyclic voltammetry treatment in 0.5 M H2SO4 in the potential range from −0.5 to +1.4 V at the scan rate of 100 mV/s until its CV curve remain unchanged.

To fabricate the aptasensor, Ag/His-GQD/G (10 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL of 1% chitosan solution by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then, its 5 μL was dropped on the surface of pretreated GCE. After it was dried in air, 5 μL of 8 μM TCEP activated aptamer was dropped on the surface of modified electrode and subsequently incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. Followed by washing with the PBS of pH 7.4 for three times to obtain the aptasensor. To block the non-specific sites, 5 μL of 1 mM MCH was added to the surface of aptasensor. The as-prepared aptasensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C when not in use.

The electrochemical experiments were carried out on CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China) in a one-compartment three-electrode cell. The modified GCE was used as working electrode, a platinum wire electrode was served as a counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as a reference electrode. All electrochemical measurements were carried out in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3 at room temperature. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) parameters were set in the range of 0–0.5 V with 50 mV s−1 scanning speed. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were completed in the frequency range 100 KHz and 1 Hz with the potential amplitude of ±5 mV. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) parameters were set to a scan rate of 4 mV s−1, 50 mV pulse amplitude, 50 ms pulse width and −0.1 V initial potential.

The acetamiprid standard solution or the same solution (5 μL) was dropped on the surface of aptasensor. After incubated at room temperature for 50 min, the aptasensor was washed by the PBS of pH 7.4 and subsequently was immersed in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3 and subjected to DPV measurement on CHI660D electrochemical workstation. The DPV responses were recorded and used for detection of acetamiprid.

Electrochemical properties of metal-graphene hybrid strongly depend on the particle size, density and dispersion of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets. However, metal nanoparticles in the hybrid prepared by present techniques are mainly distributed at the edge of graphene sheets and rarely appear on the surface of graphene sheets. The study reported one strategy for synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion (shown in Fig. 1 ). The aim of synthesis strategy is to achieve immobilization and dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on graphene sheets as well as construction of three-dimensional graphene framework. In the synthesis, histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) are firmly bound with graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) to form stable His-GQD/GO composite. To obtain Ag/His-GQD/G, it is covalently combined with Ag+ to form Ag/His-GQD/GO complex and heated in N2 atmosphere.

His-GQD plays key roles in synthesis of Ag/His-GQD/G in the strategy. His-GQDs are composed of tiny graphene sheets with size of several nanometers and the rich of hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amido and imidazole groups. The existence of hydrophilic groups makes His-GQD high water-solubility. The characteristic determines that His-GQDs can fully contact with graphene sheets in an aqueous medium. Thus, His-GQDs are uniformly dispersed on graphene sheets to form stable His-GQD/GO composite. The existence of a large π-electronconjugatedsystem in tiny graphene sheets of His-GQDs and imidazole ring makes His-GQDs can be firmly immobilized on the surface of graphene sheets from GO by the π-π stacking interaction between His-GQD and GO. In addition, His-GQDs can bind with GO at the edges of graphene sheets via Van Der Waals and hydrogen bond owing to the introduction of many hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amido at the edge of graphene sheets in His-GQD and GO. To firm the suggest, the fluorescent behavior of His-GQDs in the mixing process of His-GQDs with GO was monitored. Figure Supplementary Fig. 1 indicates that the fluorescence intensity will rapidly decrease with increasing GO. The result demonstrates that a strong interaction occurs in between His-GQD and GO. The interaction brings an energy transfer from His-GQD to GO, thus leading to a rapid fluorescence quenching. When mass ratio of GO/His-GQD is close to 0.8, the fluorescence intensity is no obvious change with increase of GO. This demonstrates that all His-GQDs have been bound on graphene sheets. The resulted His-GQD/GO composite can combine with Ag+ ions to produce stable complex via the coordination of imidazole groups in His-GQD with Ag+. This achieves to the immobilization of Ag+ on the graphene sheets and finally leads to in-situ formation of Ag nanoparticles (shown in Figure Supplementary Fig. 2). The study reveals that addition of Ag+ causes a rapid precipitation process. In the process, Ag+ ion can simultaneously coordinate with imidazole groups in His-GQD/GO composite to form corresponding complex. The coordination will cause obvious polymerization of graphene sheets. The polymerization will largely reduce the water-solubility and results in a precipitation process (see Figure Supplementary Fig. 3). In addition, the polymerization also leads to formation of three-dimensional graphene framework structure composing of interwoven graphene sheets. To obtain Ag/His-GQD/G, Ag/His-GQD/GO complex was subjected to a thermal reduction in N2 atmosphere. Under heating, the most of function groups in both GO and His-GQD will be stripped. At the same time, Ag+ ions in the complex are changed into Ag0 atoms, finally leading to formation of Ag nanocrystals. The study reveals that the choice of reduction temperature is important to realize an excellent dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on graphene sheets. Often, use of a relatively high temperature can accelerate the reduction of GO and His-GQD as well as Ag+. However, the decomposition of His-GQD produces large amount of gases under such a high temperature. The escape of these gases strips some Ag nanoparticles from graphene sheets and moves them in the direction of the airflow. This brings a poor dispersion of Ag nanoparticles in the final hybrid. To overcome the problem, a suitable temperature of 300 °C was employed for the reduction of Ag/His-GQD/GO complex.

The as-designed Ag/His-GQD/G was synthesized and its structure was characterized by SEM, TEM, IR and XRD techniques. Fig. 2 A shows that the Ag/His-GQD/G offers a well-defined three-dimensional structure. The spherical Ag nanoparticles are uniformly distributed on graphene sheets. Compared with the Ag/G made by classical method (shown in Figure Supplementary Fig. 4), Ag/His-GQD/G hybrid gives a much better dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on graphene sheets. In the Ag/G, the graphene sheets display serious agglomeration and its Ag nanoparticles are mostly distributed on the edge of graphene sheets and rarely appeared in the surface of graphene sheets. The result verifies that the introduction of His-GQD can remarkably improve the dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of graphene sheets. This is because His-GQDs immobilizes Ag nanoparticles on graphene sheets, which can effectively avoid agglomeration of Ag nanoparticles. The action makes active sites of Ag nanoparticles can fully expose to the outside and bring the improvement of catalytic activity. Moreover, the presence of His-GQDs makes the Ag nanoparticles be well embedded between different graphene sheets. The action leads to formation of three-dimensional graphene framework structure, greatly reducing the degree of aggregation of graphene sheets. This increases the active surface area and improves the catalytic activity and electron/ion conductivity during the electrochemical reaction. Further, the result of TEM analysis reveals that Ag nanoparticles were well anchored on the graphene sheets with the average size of 25 nm. On the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, there is the lattice spacing of 0.235 nm. This is corresponded to the (1 1 1) plane of Ag nanocrystals. The elemental mapping analysis shows that the hybrid contains Ag and N elements. Because Ag element only comes from the Ag nanoparticles, and N element only comes from the His-GQD. The above result verifies that our concept successfully achieves to the hybrid of graphene sheets, Ag nanoparticles and His-GQD. On the IR spectrum, there are several IR absorption peaks and bands. The peaks at 3400, 1773, 1674, 1102 and 1029 cm−1 are corresponded to IR absorption of O-H, C = O, C = C and epoxy vibration and C-O stretching vibration, respectively. On the XRD patterns there are five XRD diffraction peaks at 26°, 38.3°, 44.4°, 64.5° and 77.5°. The peak at 26° is corresponded the (0 0 2) of crystal pace of graphene material. The peaks at 38.3°, 44.4°, 64.5° and 77.5° are assigned to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) of cubic Ag crystal (JCPDS No. 65–2871). Further, oxygen content in the hybrid before and after thermal reduction were determined by elemental analysis. The result shows the thermal reduction remarkably reduced the oxygen content from 32.5% to 4.6%. The result verifies that the thermal reduction can reduce GO into graphene at 300 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h. In general, the above results have demonstrated that our synthesis concept can achieve to the fabrication of Ag/His-GQD/G with excellent dispersion.

To prove the feasibility of synthesis strategy for adjusting Ag nanoparticles, effects of His-GQD/GO and Ag+/GO mass ratios on synthesis of Ag/His-GQD/G was investigated. Fig. 3 presents SEM images of the Ag/His-GQD/G hybrids made by using different mass ratios of His-GQD/GO and the same Ag+/GO. With the increase of His-GQD/GO mass ratio, size and density of Ag nanoparticles rapidly increase. The result demonstrates that our strategy can realize adjusting Ag nanoparticles by varying the mass ratio of His-GQD/GO. When the His-GQD/GO is at a very low level, only small amounts of Ag+ ion can be immobilized on the graphene sheets. This leads to formation of sparse and small Ag nanoparticles. With increasing the mass ratio of His-GQD/GO, the amounts of Ag+ ion fixed on the graphene sheets will rapidly increase. This results in producing more Ag nanoparticles with relatively big particle size. In addition, Fig. 3 also indicates three-dimensional graphene frameworks in the hybrid. This is mainly attributed to the coordination between Ag+ and imidazole group in His-GQD. The coordination leads to the polymerization of different graphene sheets and creates the three-dimensional graphene framework networks, which are composed of interwoven graphene sheets. The three-dimensional structure helps to the improvement of ion transport during the electrochemical reaction. To better understand the effect of His-GQD/GO on the synthesis, the relationship curve of mass ratios of His-GQD/GO with particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles in Ag/His-GQD/G were calculated by based on the SEM images. Fig. 4 indicates that both particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles show a linear growth trend with the increase of His-GQD/GO mass ratio. This demonstrates that size and density of Ag nanoparticles can be effectively adjusted by varying mass ratio of His-GQD/GO.

Fig. 5 indicates the SEM images of Ag/His-GQD/G hybrids prepared by using different mass ratio of Ag+/GO and the same mass ratio of His-GQD/GO of 1.25. It can be clearly observed that no precipitation appear in the system when the mass ratio of Ag+/GO is less than 0.007. This is because only small amount of Ag+ ions was fixed on the graphene sheets. The immobilization of Ag+ ions does not cause a significant change in water-solubility, thus no precipitation was separated from the system. At a small mass ratio of Ag+/GO, the formed Ag nanoparticles offers a small particle size. Further, the SEM analysis reveals that Ag nanoparticles have the same morphology and size. Most importantly, all Ag nanoparticles are evenly distributed on the surface of graphene sheets. The result also verifies that our concept is suitable for synthesis of Ag/His-GQD/G with good dispersion even at very low content of Ag. When the mass ratio of Ag+/GO increases continuously, distinct precipitate appears in the solution (shown in Figure Supplementary Fig. 5). This is because Ag+ ions can not only coordinate with nitrogen on the one graphene sheet, but also coordinate with the nitrogen of different graphene sheets separately. Some of Ag+ ions are sandwiched between the graphene sheets, the each of graphene sheets can be connected to form a large network, which increases the molecular weight of the composite and reduces its water solubility. In addition, the increase of Ag+/GO ratio improves the three-dimensional structure. When the mass ratio of Ag+/GO reaches to 0.22, the precipitation tends to the maximum and the resulted Ag nanoparticles have the maximum particle size and density. This indicates that all His-GQDs in His-GQD/GO are involved in the coordination between Ag+ and His-GQD. Fig. 4F shows that the as-synthesized hybrid offers well-defined three-dimensional structure with excellent dispersion. The above also results demonstrate that our concept can achieve to adjust size and density of Ag nanoparticles in the hybrid by varying His-GQD/GO and Ag+/GO for the synthesis. To better understand the effect of Ag+/GO on the synthesis, the relationship curve of mass ratios of Ag/GO with particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles in Ag/His-GQD/G were calculated by based on the SEM images. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that particle size and density of Ag nanoparticles depend on the mass ratio of His-GQD/GO. With the increase of His-GQD/GO mass ratio, diameter and density of Ag nanoparticles show a linear increase. This verifies that changing the mass ratio of Ag+/GO can well adjust the size and density of Ag nanoparticles.

The electrochemical performance of Ag/His-GQD/G products prepared by using different mass ratio of Ag+/GO and the same His-GQD/GO of 1.25 to Fe(CN)6 3−/4− was investigated. Fig. 7 presents the DPV curves of modified GCE with different Ag/His-GQD/G product in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. Fig. 7 shows that the DPV peak current increases with the increase of the mass ratio Ag+/GO when the mass ratio Ag+/GO is in the range from 0.007 to 0.07. The amount of Ag nanoparticles supported on graphene sheets increased with the increase of the mass ratio Ag+/GO. This brings more active sites and higher electron conductivity, improving the catalytic activity. However, the DPV peak current began to decline when the mass ratio Ag+/GO continues to increase. This is because the use of a relatively high mass ratio of Ag+/GO may cause a serious agglomeration of Ag nanoparticles and graphene sheets in the hybrid due to high density and big size of Ag nanoparticles. This reduces the specific surface area and active sites of Ag nanoparticles and leads to a decreased DPV response. The above results further verify that electrochemical properties of metal-graphene hybrid depend on the particle size and dispersion of metal nanoparticles in graphene sheets.

Fig. 8 presents one protocol to construct on electrochemical sensing platform for detection of acetamiprid. Firstly, Ag/His-GQD/G was immobilized on GCE surface by using CS as adhesive to form one sensing film. Then, aptamer of acetamiprid was covalently linked to Ag nanoparticles in Ag/His-GQD/G via Ag-S bond. Finally, 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) was used for blocking the surface of Ag nanoparticles to avoid producing nonspecific binding. In the protocol, Ag/His-GQD/G was used as a sensing material for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid. Ag/His-GQD/G offers a well-defined three-dimensional networks structure. All Ag nanoparticles are well dispersed in the interwoven graphene networks. The combination of graphene with Ag nanoparticles gives the rich of active sites for the electrode reaction, high electrical conductivity and significant synergistic catalysis. The three-dimensional structure of Ag/His-GQD/G helps to accelerate the electrolyte transport. These factors will greatly improve the sensitivity for detection of acetamiprid. The aptamer was used as the probe to achieve specific recognition of acetamiprid. Due to ultrahigh electrocatalytic activity of Ag/His-GQD/G towards Fe(CN)6 3−/4−, the recognition produces a sensitive DPV response.

To understand the aptasensor fabrication, CV and EIS performances of different electrodes were investigated, respectively. The CV and EIS measurements were carried out in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. Fig. 9
A indicates the CV curves of different electrodes. The bare GCE displays one pair of well-defined redox peaks. After the electrode was modified with Ag/His-GQD/G, the peak current will obviously increase, implying a faster electrode reaction. This is because Ag/His-GQD/G accelerates redox of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− on the electrode surface. After the aptamer was immobilized on the electrode surface, the peak current will significantly decrease. The aptamer withnegatively charged skeleton inhibits diffusion of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− between electrode and electrolyte. The result also proves that the aptamer has been successfully immobilized the electrode surface. Because the most of active sites on the sensing material were blocked, the modification of MCH further reduces the CV peak current. Fig. 9B presents AC impedance spectra of different electrodes. The EIS includes a semicircle portion and a linear portion. The semicircle portion at higher frequencies corresponds to electron transfer limited process. The linear portion at lower frequencies represents the diffusion-limited process. The semicircle diameter equals the electron-transfer resistance (Rct). For bare GCE, its Rct was 600.8 Ω. After Ag/His-GQD/G was modified on the GCE surface, a straight line can be observed, indicating that Ag/His-GQD/G was a conductor for accelerating the electron transfer. The connection of aptamer with Ag/His-GQD/G leads to an obvious increased Rct value. The demonstrates that the aptamer partly obstructed the electron transfer. The blocking of MCH further increases the Rct value. The results of CV and EIS analysis prove that our protocol can construct on the aptasensor for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid.

To test the electrochemical response of the aptasensor towards acetamiprid, the CV and DPV curves of aptasensor incubated in 1 pM acetamiprid standard solution were measured in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. Fig. 10 shows that the incubation in the acetamiprid solution results in the decrease in the peak current of both CV and DPV. This is because the incubation leads to the formation of acetamiprid-aptamer complex on the electrode surface. The existence of the complex layer partly embarrasses the electron transfer between the electrode and the Fe(CN)6 3−/4− on the electrode surface, leading to the reduce of CV and DPV peak current. In addition, the change of signal generated by DPV was significantly greater than CV. Thus, DPV technique was selected for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid to obtain high sensitivity.

To optimize detection conditions, influence of aptamer concentration and incubation time on DPV response to 1 pM acetamiprid were investigated in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. As shown in Fig. 11 A, the DPV peak current change before and after incubated in the acetamiprid (ΔIp) increases with the increase of aptamer concentration. This is because with the increase of aptamer more acetamiprid molecules were combined with the aptamer on the electrode surface. The formation of more aptamer-acetamiprid composite brings a slower electron/ion transfer rate between electrode and electrolyte. The action leads to a smaller peak current and a bigger ΔIp value. However, the ΔIp value reaches the maximum when the aptamer concentration is 8 μM. When the aptamer concentration continues to increase, the ΔIp value is almost unchanged. The result proves that number of aptamer on the electrode surface are enough to capture all acetamiprid molecules in the reaction system. Fig. 11B presents the relationship curve of ΔIp with incubation time. The ΔIp value increases with the increasing of incubation time in the range from 25 to 50 min. Often, the hybridization of aptamer with acetamiprid needs a relatively long incubation time. A more incubation time is beneficial to full capture the acetamiprid molecules in the system. This will result in an increased ΔIp value. When the incubation time is close to 50 min, the capture process tends to complete. Therefore, the ΔIp reaches to its maximum and then remain stable. To obtain high sensitivity, the aptamer concentration of 8 μM and incubation time of 50 min were selected for the aptasensor preparation.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the DPV behavior of proposed aptasensor at different concentrations of acetamiprid was studied in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. Fig. 12 shows that the peak current (Ip) decreases with the rise of acetamiprid concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−165.0 × 10−12 M . The linear regression equation is Ip (μA) = 1.2472Log [Cacetamiprid, fM] − 10.22, the correlation coefficient 0.9972, and the detection limit of 4.0 × 10−17 M (LOD, S/N = 3). The aptasensor based Ag/His-GQD/G shows a much higher sensitivity for detection of acetamiprid compared with single Ag/His-GQD (2.7 × 10−12 M) and single Ag nanoparticles (5.3 × 10−9 M). The high sensitivity is mainly attributed to the unique structure of Ag/His-GQD/G. To understand the suggest, another Ag/His-GQD/G product was prepared by simple physical mixing of graphene, His-GQD and Ag nanoparticles and used for fabrication of the aptasensor. The investigation reveals that the aptasensor gives the detection of 8.2 × 10−13 M for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid, indicating a poor sensitivity. The result verifies that the Ag/His-GQD/G prepared by the proposed synthesis strategy has a much better catalytic activity towards Fe(CN)6 3−/4− compared with the Ag/His-GQD/G made via one simple mixing procedure. In addition, the sensitivity is much better than that of other analytical technologies for acetamiprid in Table 1 . By our strategy, the as-synthesized Ag/His-GQD/G has a well-defined three-dimensional structure that all Ag nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the surface of graphene sheets. The synthesis makes full contact and excellent dispersion of graphene sheets and Ag nanoparticles. The structural characteristic is conducive to achieving significantly synergy between graphene and Ag nanoparticles and leads to an obviously improved sensitivity for detection of acetamiprid.

The reproducibility of aptasensor was studied by analyzing DPV response of five constructed aptasensors at same concentration acetamiprid of 5.0 × 10−14 M. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.7%, indicating a good reproducibility. The stability of aptasensor also was a very important factor in practical application. The constructed aptasensor was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for 15 days. Its peak current can remain at 96.2% of initial value, indicating a good stability.

To evaluate the selectivity, the DPV peak current change (ΔIp) of proposed aptasensor before and after incubated in 5.0 × 10−14 M acetamiprid were studied in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− in the PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KNO3. At the same time, the constructed aptasensor was used to determine the same concentration of other pesticides that may interference with acetamiprid (ACE) detect, such as methyl parathion (PAR), imidacloprid (IMI), carbaryl (CAR) and pentachlorophenol (PEN). Fig. 13 indicates that the ΔIp value caused by the interference is lower than that of acetamiprid. The result verifies that the proposed aptasensor has good specificity. The good specificity is due to the specific recognition of the aptamer with acetamiprid. The other pesticides don’t produce the specific recognition with the aptamer, which will not cause changes in DPV response.

To evaluate the practicability, the as-proposed aptasensor was applied in the detection of trace acetamiprid in several tea samples. All samples were purchased from the local supermarket in Wuxi city. The pre-treatment of tea was carried out by the reported method [43]. The extracted solution was diluted to 500 mL with cyclohexane. The obtained sample solution was used for detection of acetamiprid. In addition, the recovery test was also carried out by adding the amounts of acetamiprid standard solution. The results are shown in Table 2 . It can be seen from Table 2 that the recoveries for the acetamiprid were between 92.0% and 104.2%, indicating a good accuracy.

The study has demonstrated one strategy for synthesis of silver-graphene hybrid with controllable size and excellent dispersion. The use of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots achieves to immobilization and dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on graphene sheets. The as-synthesized hybrid offers a well-defined three-dimensional structure. All Ag nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the interwoven graphene networks. More importantly, the synthesis strategy can effectively adjust the size and density of Ag nanoparticles in the hybrid by varying the mass radio of graphene quantum dot/graphene oxide or Ag+/graphene oxide. The unique structure of hybrid creates high electron/ion conductivity and synergistic electrocatalytic effect. The aptasensor based on hybrid exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, specificity and stability for electrochemical detection of acetamiprid. The synthesis strategy can be also applied in fabrication of other metal-graphene hybrids with controllable size and excellent dispersion, which meets the needs of further applications in the future.
Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology. Zhu Haiyan: Validation, Supervision. Gu Zhiguo: Validation. Wang Guangli: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145628.

ce:section.ce:para:
